title: Coordinated Inattention and Disclosure Complexity
abstract: We examine a beauty contest game with an option to analyze an additional disclosure. We analytically prove that in some scenarios, coordination incentives cause sophisticated players who can comprehend disclosures to choose not to analyze them to match unsophisticated players’ actions, a phenomenon we call “coordinated inattention.” Laboratory experiments provide support for the coordinated inattention mechanism: Coordination incentives reduce sophisticated subjects’ propensity to analyze disclosures, especially when they believe others are unlikely to comprehend them. We further find that psychological biases help reduce coordinated inattention. Subjects are overconfident, sophisticated subjects overestimate others’ ability to comprehend disclosures, and both biases are associated with a higher tendency to analyze disclosures. Our analysis suggests that unsophisticated decision makers’ inability to comprehend complex disclosures has a negative spillover effect by reducing sophisticated decision makers’ attention to disclosures. Our results highlight the importance of the recent efforts of the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and the Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB) to make disclosures easier to comprehend.

article.1. Introduction: Accounting disclosures contain critical value-relevant information. However, studies show that information users—including sophisticated ones such as institutions and financial analysts—often fail to fully utilize the contents of disclosures when making decisions (Hirshleifer et al. 2008,Blankespoor et al. 2019). It is well known that decision makers might rationally choose to ignore disclosures for two reasons. First, disclosures are costly to analyze. AsBlankespoor et al. (2020)note, these costs include the effort to extract quantitative and qualitative information from the disclosure. Second, disclosures can be complex and difficult for information users to comprehend; if a user spends time analyzing a disclosure but is unable to comprehend it, the disclosure analysis is not worthwhile. Simple frictions such as the limited capacity to analyze disclosures can explain why unsophisticated information users do not pay attention to accounting disclosures (Bertomeu et al. 2023,Mackowiak and Matejka 2023), yet it is less clear why sophisticated agents, who can comprehend the disclosures, would choose to ignore them when the disclosures are readily available. When there are incentives to match others’ actions, sophisticated information users might strategically choose to ignore disclosures to coordinate with unsophisticated ones, a phenomenon we call “coordinated inattention.”1 We begin by developing a theoretical framework to formally analyze coordinated inattention. We followMorris and Shin (2002)in modeling coordination incentives as a beauty contest game whereby players are incentivized to choose an action that is close to both a “fundamental” and others’ actions. The coordination incentive is weak (strong) when players are incentivized to choose actions that are relatively closer to the fundamental (others’ actions). Before playing the beauty contest game, players have the option to analyze a disclosure about the fundamental at a cost. Players make this disclosure analysis decision simultaneously. The probability of successfully comprehending the disclosure varies among players, which captures the idea that information users are heterogeneous in their ability to comprehend disclosures. As disclosures become more (less) technical, players’ ability to comprehend them decreases (increases). The key idea of coordinated inattention is that unsophisticated players’ inability to comprehend disclosures can cause sophisticated players to rationally choose to ignore disclosures, because by doing so, they can closely match the unsophisticated players’ actions without incurring the cost of analyzing the disclosure. In addition to formalizing this intuition, our model generates predictions for the impact of disclosure analysis costs and the technicality of disclosure on coordinated inattention. To empirically analyze coordinated inattention, we need to observe whether sophisticated investors strategically choose to ignore public disclosures and whether this decision is affected by their beliefs about others’ ability to process disclosures. Because such information is generally not observable using archival data, we conduct laboratory experiments to examine coordinated inattention. In the experiments, we manipulate the coordination incentives, the technicality of the disclosure, and the cost of analyzing the disclosure. Specifically, we employ a 2 (high coordination/no coordination) × 2 (technical/nontechnical) × 2 (high cost/low cost) experimental design. Subjects choose whether to analyze a disclosure by incurring a cost before they play the baseline game. In the condition without coordination incentives, subjects are only incentivized to choose actions close to an unknown fundamental. With coordination incentives, subjects are motivated to choose actions close to their matched partner’s action as well as the fundamental. If a subject chooses to analyze the disclosure, a math problem is shown to the subject. If the subject correctly solves the math problem, the fundamental is revealed. We vary the technicality of the disclosure by changing the difficulty of the math problems as inCarlin et al. (2013): in the nontechnical condition, the math problems are much easier to solve, and thus subjects are more likely to observe the fundamental relative to the technical condition. Finally, we vary the disclosure analysis cost by manipulating the cost that subjects must pay to observe the math problem. Our experimental results are mostly consistent with the model’s predictions. We take the case without coordination incentives, where theory predicts that sophisticated players will analyze the disclosure, as a benchmark. By introducing a high level of coordination incentives, theory predicts coordinated inattention to be the unique equilibrium when the disclosure is technical or the cost is high. We find that coordination incentives drastically reduce disclosure analysis in such scenarios. Moreover, controlling for a subject’s own ability to comprehend a disclosure, we find that they are more likely to ignore disclosures when they believe that others are unable to comprehend them. In addition, when the disclosure is nontechnical and the cost is low, the model predicts that two different types of equilibria can arise—one with coordinated inattention and the other with disclosure analysis by sophisticated players. In this treatment, we find no evidence of coordinated inattention in that coordination incentives do not significantly reduce disclosure analysis. Overall, our results suggest that coordinated inattention only arises when it is the unique equilibrium. Although we observe a dramatic reduction in disclosure analysis (relative to the no-coordination incentive benchmark) when theory predicts coordinated inattention, we still find that a nonnegligible number of subjects analyze disclosure in such cases. To understand why, we consider players’ beliefs about their own and others’ ability. Before subjects play the coordination game, they complete 10 math questions like the ones in the coordination game. After completing these questions, subjects are asked to estimate the percentage of questions that they and others answered correctly. We find that subjects overestimate their own ability, consistent with overconfidence.2In addition, there is a very high correlation between players’ own ability to solve the problems and their predictions about the percentage of others who solve the same problems correctly, consistent with a false consensus effect.3Both of these violations of standard economic assumptions of well-calibrated beliefs should lead to more disclosure analysis than predicted by theory. Intuitively, if people overestimate their own ability, they will be more willing to incur the cost of analyzing a disclosure. A false consensus can cause sophisticated players to mistakenly believe that disclosures are easy for everyone to analyze, that is, it can cause sophisticated players to believe the technicality of disclosure is low when it is actually high. Consistent with this idea, we find that sophisticated players who believe that others are also sophisticated are significantly more likely to analyze disclosures than other sophisticated players. We contribute to the literature examining investors’ inattention to disclosures (Blankespoor et al. 2020). Coordinated inattention provides a rational explanation for investor inattention. Other researchers in the rational inattention literature also seek to analyze the rational decisions to ignore disclosures (Peng and Xiong 2006,Bertomeu et al. 2023,Mackowiak and Matejka 2023). Investors in these models have exogenous information capacity constraints and rationally choose how to allocate their attention. In rational inattention models, the cost of processing information is the friction that leads to investor inattention. In contrast, sophisticated information users’ belief about others’ ability to comprehend disclosures is the main driver of inattention in our setting with the strategic motivation to match others’ actions. Coordination incentives are not only relevant in the original metaphor of the stock market as a beauty contest; they are also prevalent in credit markets. Indeed, financial economists have documented coordination incentives in lending markets, where failure to coordinate can lead to premature foreclosure on borrowers (Hertzberg et al. 2011). Accounting disclosures are an important source of public information for investors, creditors, and firms to make important decisions that potentially involve some degree of coordination incentives, and these disclosures have become increasingly more complex. Our study provides new insights about the potential consequence of this increased complexity for information users’ decisions to analyze accounting disclosures. Our analysis of coordinated inattention has significant policy implications. Stakeholders have long argued that accounting standards are overly complex. In response, FASB officially launched a simplification initiative in 2014, with projects “intended to improve or maintain the usefulness of the information reported to investors while reducing costs and complexity in financial reporting.” (FASB 2014).4FASB successfully completed several narrow-scope projects as part of the 2014 simplification initiative, addressing topics such as removing extraordinary items (Accounting Standards Update (ASU) 2015-01), simplifying accounting for debt issuance costs (ASU 2015-03), and employee share-based payment accounting (ASU1016-09), among others. Some critics express concerns that these simplifications might reduce the usefulness of financial reporting (Singh et al. 2015). However, our study suggests these simplifications can increase the use of accounting information by preventing coordinated inattention. Reducing the technicality of the disclosures makes them more comprehensible to unsophisticated users and reduces the time required for analysis, which should increase disclosure analysis by sophisticated users. This increase in disclosure analysis should presumably lead to better decisions and increase the usefulness of accounting disclosures compared with the alternative scenario where they are ignored.
article.2. Related Literature: Our study is related to the literature on disclosure complexity.Dyer et al. (2017)examine 10-K filings over the period 1996–2013 and find that these disclosures have increased in length and redundancy and have decreased in readability. They further find that the accounting standard complexity rules introduced by recent FASB and Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) requirements explain most of the increase in length. As accounting standards become more complex, disclosures contain more jargon that is harder for people without accounting expertise to understand.Bonsall et al. (2017)measure the readability of 10-K filings using an index that captures the plain-English attributes of disclosures such as the use of an active voice. They find that less readable 10-K reports are associated with greater stock return volatility following the filing, which is consistent with the idea that complexity makes it more difficult for information users to find decision-relevant information.Hoitash and Hoitash (2018)use the count of the number of accounting items (XBRL tags) disclosed in 10-K filings to create a measure of accounting reporting complexity. They document that their measure of accounting complexity is associated with misstatements, audit delays, and higher audit fees. Recent analytical and experimental studies have examined the strategic choice of disclosure complexity from the perspective of information providers.Chen et al. (2020)jointly analyze firms’ effort to decrease disclosure complexity and information users’ effort to analyze disclosures. Firms’ choice to improve disclosure accessibility is similar to our manipulation of disclosure complexity in that investors’ likelihood of comprehending a disclosure decreases in a disclosure’s complexity, holding the information content of the disclosure constant.Aghamolla and Smith (2023)consider the strategic choice of disclosure complexity by managers with mixed motives: Managers may choose to increase the complexity of a disclosure either to provide more information or to obfuscate. A key assumption shared by our paper andAghamolla and Smith (2023)is that information users are heterogeneous in their ability to process disclosures. An experimental study byJin et al. (2022)focuses on the obfuscation motive underlying the model ofAghamolla and Smith (2023). They find that managers choose high levels of complexity to obfuscate, but investors are unable to see through this strategic complexity. They also find that information users are overconfident about their ability to process complex information. Our study is closely related to the literature on beauty contest games. Prior analytical studies such asHellwig and Veldkamp (2009)andMyatt and Wallace (2012)have explored the effect of coordination incentives on information acquisition, but they impose the assumption that players are homogeneous in their ability to comprehend disclosures. Most prior experiments on beauty contest games do not consider endogenous information acquisition. Recent experiments byBanerjee et al. (2022)test a simplified model followingMyatt and Wallace (2012), but they provide information directly to subjects and do not consider heterogeneity in players’ ability to comprehend disclosures.5Our experiments support the idea that people are heterogeneous in their comprehension ability. In addition, we document systematic biases in players’ beliefs about their own and others’ ability to comprehend disclosures, and we examine how these biases relate to their propensity to analyze disclosures. Our experimental analysis suggests that coordinated inattention is less likely to arise than suggested by theory. First, when multiple equilibria can arise, we find that coordinated inattention does not occur. Second, we find strong evidence that sophisticated information users overestimate others’ ability, which can prevent coordinated inattention from arising.

Experimental Parameter and Predictions In the experiment we use the following set of parameters and variable distributions: Based on these specifications, it is easily verified thatVar⁡[𝜃|𝑦]=2500/⁢3Var⁡[θ|y]=2500/⁢3and𝐄⁡[𝜃|𝑦]=𝑦E⁡[θ|y]=y. Subjects choose to pay a cost,C, to see the math problem. We reveal the fundamental to them if they solve the math problem correctly; otherwise, subjects do not observe any additional information. We manipulate three factors: (1) disclosure technicality, (2) the cost of analyzing the disclosure, and (3) coordination incentives. To vary the technicality of the disclosure, we manipulate the difficulty of the math problems in a similar manner asCarlin et al. (2013). This treatment is interesting to study experimentally because theory does not provide a clear prediction regarding the realized equilibrium. 4.2. Experimental Procedures We conducted two sessions for each treatment (16 sessions in total) using a computer program written in Java at a computer laboratory in a university. Each session had 12 participants. In total, 192 student subjects (undergraduate and graduate) participated in 16 sessions. Participants were given instructions to read, and they completed quiz questions to test their understanding of the instructions. The experimenter then reviewed the instructions. We provide a summary of the experimental sessions inTable 1. Table 1.Summary of Experimental Sessions Table 1.Summary of Experimental Sessions Note.Abbreviations: HC, high cost; LC, low cost; T, technical; NT, nontechnical. Each experimental session had two parts. In the first part, participants solved 10 math problems, one at a time. They had one minute to solve each problem, and received 100 experimental francs for each problem they solved correctly. At the end of this part, each participant estimated the number of questions they correctly solved and the average number of questions that other participants correctly solved. They earned 100 experimental francs if their estimates were within 10% of the actual percentage, and they received feedback about their performance and the average performance of other subjects. Next, participants repeatedly played a two-stage game. In the first stage, a public clue was announced, which is equal to the fundamental plus an error term. In addition, participants chose whether to incur a cost in order to analyze a disclosure about the fundamental.12If players chose to incur the cost, a math problem (similar to the ones they solved in the first part) was shown on their screen. If they correctly solved the math problem within a minute, the true value of the fundamental was revealed to them. If they decided not to incur the cost to analyze the disclosure or failed to solve the problem, the only information they had was the public clue. In the second stage, participants made their action choices. After each round, each participant received feedback about the partner’s choice of incurring the cost to analyze the disclosure, whether the partner solved the math problem correctly (if the partner chose to incur the cost), and the subject’s own payoff. After the 15 rounds of the experiment, subjects played the game for an additional 15 rounds with the opposite coordination incentives: the subjects in the𝜌=0ρ=0condition played 15 rounds with𝜌=0.8ρ=0.8, and the subjects in the𝜌=0.8ρ=0.8condition played 15 rounds with𝜌=0ρ=0. In our main analysis, we focus on the first 15 rounds. We restrict attention to the first 15 rounds because of the strong presence of order effects whereby subjects who start with low coordination incentives and switch to high coordination incentives behave much differently than those who play the conditions in the opposite order, as we discuss in further detail in Section B.3 of the electronic companion. Moreover, in Sections B.4 and B.5 of the electronic companion, we show the results of our tests when the sample is restricted to rounds 16–30 and when all 30 rounds are considered, respectively. Participants were rematched in each round within their group. We generated 15 independent fundamental values (𝜃θ) and one public clue (y) for each fundamental value. The same set of fundamental values and clues were used in all experimental sessions, as shown in Online Appendix Table B.1. At the end of the experiment, participants completed a postexperimental questionnaire. They were paid in cash after successfully completing the session based on their performance, which was measured in the fictitious currency (experimental francs) and converted to U.S. dollars at a rate of $1 for every 400 experimental francs. article.5. Experimental Results: 5.1. Hypothesis Testing According toHypotheses 1and2, coordination incentives will lead to less disclosure analysis when the disclosure has a high cost or the disclosure is technical. We test this prediction in panels (a) and (b) ofFigure 4by plotting the percentage of times that a disclosure is analyzed for each of the eight experimental conditions: {technical versus non-technical disclosure} × {high versus low cost} × {high versus low coordination incentives}. Inspecting these two panels, it is clear that our hypotheses are confirmed: When disclosure is technical (Figure 4(a)), there is significantly less disclosure analysis when coordination incentives are high (represented by the orange bars, 13.1% and 5.8% when the cost is low and high, respectively) than when the coordination incentives are low (represented by the blue bars, 48.3% and 43.1% when the cost is low and high, respectively). Moreover, when disclosure is nontechnical (Figure 4(b)), coordination incentives significantly reduce disclosure analysis when the cost is high (the right pair of bars, indicating a reduction from 72.2% to 25.3%). Recall fromFigure 3that the (nontechnical disclosure, low cost, high coordination incentives) treatment is the one where multiple equilibria can be supported. Empirically, of the four technicality × cost conditions, only the (nontechnical, low cost) condition exhibits similar levels of disclosure analysis (80.1% and 76.4%) across the two levels of coordination incentives. Thus, it appears that when multiple equilibria can be supported, coordinated attention is the one that arises.13 5.2. The Coordinated Inattention Mechanism The main idea behind coordinated inattention is that people analyze less disclosure (controlling for difficulty and cost) when they expect others to be ignorant of the disclosure. The laboratory allows us to directly examine the underlying mechanism behind coordinated inattention. In our experiments, we obtain natural variation in the perceived difficulty of the partner’s task through the heterogeneity in subjects’ beliefs about others’ ability.14Thus, we can conduct additional tests of coordinated inattention by examining how the effect of coordination incentives on the propensity to analyze disclosures is related to subjects’ beliefs about others’ ability to correctly solve the math questions. Specifically, we can examine whether the effect of coordination incentives on the propensity to analyze a disclosure is greater (in magnitude) when, controlling for the subject’s own ability, the subject is pessimistic about others’ ability to comprehend the disclosure. To test this, we consider both of the technicality conditions (technical and nontechnical), but we restrict the sample to subjects who are able to correctly solve at least 50% of the math questions correctly in the math stage of the experiment.15This results in a sample of 129 subjects, 36 (93) of whom are in a treatment with technical (nontechnical) disclosure. We begin by simply computing these subjects’ propensity to analyze disclosure as a function of the cost of analyzing the disclosure, the coordination incentives, and subject’ beliefs about others’ ability to correctly solve the math questions. In this first test, we classify subjects as believing that other subjects are sophisticated if their estimate for the percentage of questions that others answered correctly in the math stage of the experiment is greater than 73%, which is the average percentage of the sophisticated subjects in the technical condition (see the second row, first column of Panel B ofTable 4). We report these results in inTable 2. Psychological Traits and Learning Perhaps the most apparent off-equilibrium behavior is that there is a nonnegligible amount of disclosure analysis even when coordinated inattention is the unique equilibrium: Subjects choose to analyze the disclosure 14.7% of the time when coordinated inattention is the unique equilibrium. In the model, there is common knowledge about other players’ ex ante likelihood of understanding a disclosure, and players’ beliefs about others’ ability to comprehend a disclosure is entirely determined by the technicality of the disclosure. In practice and in the laboratory, however, this common knowledge of players’ ability is likely to be violated. Recall that prior to playing the game, each subject was asked to answer 10 math questions. Subjects in the technical (nontechnical) treatments were given relatively difficult (easy) questions. When developing our model inSection 3, we invoked the standard assumption that agents correctly assess their own and others’ ability. There are good reasons to question this assumption. Regarding people’s perception of their own ability, psychologists have shown that people tend to be overconfident in that they often overestimate their own ability. In addition, psychologists have documented a false consensus effect whereby people tend to overestimate their similarity to others. Thus, high (low)-ability subjects might be expected to overestimate (underestimate) the ability of others, in which case people’s beliefs about others’ ability will be biased. We asked subjects to predict their own and others’ accuracy in solving the math problems. We summarize these findings inTable 4. When disclosure is technical, subjects estimate they can answer 43.5% of the questions correctly, but they are only able to answer the questions correctly 37.6% of the time. This difference (5.9%) is statistically significant (𝑡=2.70t=2.70), indicating that subjects tend to overestimate their own ability, consistent with overconfidence. Subjects also overestimate their ability in the nontechnical treatments (90.9% estimate versus 87.9% actual), but this difference (3.0%) is not as significant economically or statistically (𝑡=1.98t=1.98). Conversely, in the treatments with nontechnical disclosure, subjects underestimate others’ ability to solve the problems correctly: they estimate that others can solve the questions correctly 81.9% of the time, when in reality, they are able to solve the questions correctly 87.9% of the time. This difference is also statistically significant (𝑡=3.70t=3.70). Next, we separately report the beliefs of sophisticated and unsophisticated subjects in the technical treatment. We repeat the analysis from Panel A, except we (i) restrict attention to the experimental treatments where the disclosure is technical and (ii) separately consider unsophisticated players (who correctly solve fewer than half of the math problems in the math stage) and sophisticated players (who correctly solve at least half of the math problems in the math stage). We find that sophisticated players slightly overestimate the percentage of questions they answer correctly (77.4% prediction versus 73.1% actual), but this difference is not statistically significant (𝑡=1.17t=1.17).19Unsophisticated players, on the other hand, significantly overestimate their ability (23.2% prediction versus 16.3% actual,𝑡=2.52t=2.52). We next consider players’ beliefs about others’ ability. Consistent with a false consensus effect, sophisticated players severely overestimate others’ ability: They estimate that others can solve the math questions correctly 70.3% of the time, when in reality, the other subjects are only able to solve the problems correctly 37.4% of the time, a difference that is highly statistically significant (𝑡=9.53t=9.53). Unsophisticated players, on the other hand, have mostly unbiased beliefs about others’ ability: they estimate that others can solve the math questions correctly 42.1% of the time, which is only slightly greater than the actual (37.7%), a difference that is not statistically significantly different from zero (𝑡=1.36t=1.36). Summarizing the key observations from Panel B: Sophisticated players have mostly unbiased beliefs about their own ability, but unsophisticated players overestimate their own ability. Sophisticated players drastically overestimate others’ ability, whereas unsophisticated people have mostly unbiased beliefs about others’ ability. Taken together, these biases are likely to lead to more disclosure analysis than what is predicted by theory: Sophisticated players might choose to analyze technical disclosures when coordinated inattention is the unique equilibrium because they mistakenly believe that others will be able to comprehend the disclosures, too; essentially, this scenario would correspond to sophisticated players mistakenly believing that the disclosure is nontechnical, when in reality it is technical.20Moreover, unsophisticated players, who should rationally choose not to analyze disclosures when they are technical, might choose to analyze a disclosure because they mistakenly believe they will be able to comprehend it. To formally examine the relation between the false consensus effect, overconfidence, and off-equilibrium behavior, we run regressions on the sample of subjects who participated in treatments with high coordination incentives and technical disclosures, a setting where theory predicts coordinated inattention to be the unique equilibrium.21In column (1) ofTable 5, we regress the proportion of times that a subject chooses to analyze the disclosure onto an indicator for the subject believing that other subjects are sophisticated, specifically, an indicator for the subject estimating that other subjects were able to answer at least 73% of the math questions correctly in the math stage of the experiment.22We find that, controlling for ability and disclosure costs, subjects who believe that others are sophisticated are 25 percentage points more likely to analyze disclosure than those who believe that others are unsophisticated. In columns (2)–(3), we separate the sample into those who are sophisticated (column (2)) and unsophisticated (column (3)).23The effect is concentrated among sophisticated subjects: Sophisticated subjects (column (2)) who believe others are sophisticated are 40.3 percentage points more likely to analyze disclosure than sophisticated subjects who do not believe that others are sophisticated. Economically, this effect is even larger than the effect of the disclosure cost (35.3 percentage points). In contrast, unsophisticated subjects’ beliefs about others’ ability have little impact on their propensity to analyze disclosure (column (3)). Thus, although psychological effects like the false consensus effect and overconfidence might prevent coordinated inattention from arising in the short run, in the long run, information users’ behavior is likely to converge toward coordinated inattention when there are coordination incentives and disclosures are either overly technical or costly for users to analyze. article.6. Conclusion: Studies have found that sophisticated users who can comprehend disclosures do not fully utilize them (Hirshleifer et al. 2008,Blankespoor et al. 2019). We show that coordination incentives can explain why sophisticated decision makers ignore disclosures, and we highlight the negative externality that unsophisticated decision makers can create on other, more sophisticated players’ decisions to analyze disclosures. Specifically, we show that “coordinated inattention” can arise because sophisticated decision makers, who are likely to comprehend the disclosure, rationally ignore it so that they can match the actions of unsophisticated decision makers who are unlikely to comprehend the disclosure. We conduct laboratory experiments and confirm that sophisticated subjects are less likely to analyze disclosures when they believe that others are less likely to comprehend them. However, there is still some analysis of disclosures even when theory predicts there to be none. To understand why some subjects choose to analyze disclosures when theory predicts otherwise, we analyze subjects’ beliefs about their own and others’ ability to comprehend disclosures. This behavioral analysis reveals two key findings. First, subjects overestimate their ability to comprehend disclosures, consistent with overconfidence. Second, sophisticated subjects (who can comprehend disclosures) overestimate the likelihood that others can, consistent with a false consensus effect. We show that both of these psychological traits are associated with an increased tendency for subjects to analyze disclosures, which helps explain the deviations from the theoretical predictions and the behavior that we observe in the laboratory. We adopt simplified assumptions in our model to make the experiments easy for subjects to understand. Relaxing these assumptions can be avenues for future research. For example, we capture strategic complementarity by imposing an exogenous coordination incentive in a beauty contest game. Future studies can study information acquisition decisions in settings where coordination incentives arise endogenously (e.g.,Allen et al. 2006).
